note_on_holiday: June 19 (Juneteenth) is outside this period; no holiday adjustments needed for Jun 10-17
macro_regime_summary: Soft CPI print on June 11 (0.1% MoM vs 0.2% est, 2.4% YoY) provided rate-cut optionism that lifted tech. AVGO post-earnings momentum (reported June 5: Q2 AI rev +46% YoY, record $15B total rev). The dominant macro shock of the period was Israel's June 13 military strikes on Iran (Operation opening 200 jets, killing top IRGC leadership + nuclear scientists) — Brent crude jumped 7% on June 13, USO went from ~71.6 to 82.3 (+14.9% in 7 days). Gold surged on safe-haven demand (GLD: 306→316). Small caps underperformed throughout. AMD had a spectacular gap-up on June 16 (+8.8%) on easing of China GPU export restrictions (MI308 license approvals). Overall S&P for the period: roughly flat (-1% from Jun10 high of 596 to Jun17 590), but June month ended +5.1% to all-time high 6,205.

trades.thesis: AVGO reported blowout Q2 FY2025 earnings on June 5 (reported after close): record $15B revenue (+20% YoY), AI revenue $4.4B (+46% YoY), Q3 guidance of $15.8B (+21% YoY) with AI rev expected to accelerate to $5.1B. Stock was still running on the earnings gap — a classic post-earnings drift setup. CPI print on June 11 expected to be soft (consensus 0.2%), which would further lift rate-sensitive AI names. AVGO is the highest-quality AI beneficiary with custom chip moat (XPU revenue growing faster than NVDA). Entry into the post-earnings drift with clear catalyst ahead.
trades.catalyst: Post-Q2 FY2025 earnings drift (reported June 5). Imminent soft CPI print anticipated. AI networking demand re-rating. Custom silicon TAM expansion narrative.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AVGO was legitimately the best risk/reward in the AI complex post-earnings. Soft CPI on June 11 was the short-term catalyst. Then AVGO continued grinding higher as AI infrastructure spending narrative remained intact, reaching $274 by June 30. The 12-Day War (Israel-Iran from June 13) barely dented AVGO — it dipped to $246.7 on June 13 but recovered to $250 by June 16, showing strong hands. The real driver was the market repricing the custom AI chip TAM.
trades.lesson: Post-earnings drift on quality AI names with accelerating revenue and raising guidance is one of the most reliable setups. AVGO had the trifecta: beat + raise + accelerating AI revenue growth. The stock absorbed the Israel-Iran shock (-3.2% on June 13) and immediately recovered — that relative strength during a macro shock is a strong signal of genuine institutional accumulation. Target was too conservative at $265; the stock ran to $274 in 20 days. Lesson: when AI capex cycle is intact and company is beating estimates by meaningful margins, extend target to 1.5x the implied move.

trades.thesis: AAPL underperforming tech peers significantly — while NVDA, AVGO, MSFT running, AAPL was flat-to-down. The stock had no AI revenue story (hardware cycles, not cloud AI infrastructure). WWDC concerns — Apple Intelligence rollout slow, no hardware upgrade cycle catalyst near-term. Expensive at ~30x on sub-10% earnings growth. In a risk-on AI rotation, capital was leaving defensively-priced mega-cap hardware toward AI pure-plays. Short AAPL as a pairs trade (fund the AVGO long) or outright short.
trades.catalyst: Sector rotation out of non-AI mega-cap tech. WWDC 2025 overhang. No near-term product cycle catalyst. Relative weakness vs peers. China revenue risk from ongoing US-China trade tensions.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AAPL dropped from $201.87 to $194.87 by June 17 (-3.5%). The stock was clearly weak relative to the broader market. The Israel-Iran shock on June 13 hit AAPL harder than AI names (down to $195.67 on June 13 from $197.99 on June 11). AAPL had no AI infrastructure tailwind to cushion the geopolitical selloff. Closed short on June 13 at $195.67 for a quick +3.1%.
trades.lesson: In an AI-driven bull market, identify the mega-caps that are NOT participating — they are natural shorts or pairs legs. AAPL in June 2025 had no AI revenue inflection, no product cycle, and was expensive relative to peers with actual AI growth. The short worked for two reasons: (1) sector rotation headwind, (2) AAPL has China supply chain exposure which got re-priced during Israel-Iran escalation. Cover quickly on geopolitical events as AAPL can gap back fast on unrelated macro relief (Fed, trade deals).

trades.thesis: Cool CPI print on June 11 (0.1% MoM vs 0.2% est, 2.4% YoY — BELOW expectations). Core CPI also 0.1% vs 0.2% consensus. This was a meaningful downside surprise that would push yields down and TLT up. Fed is on hold at 4.25-4.5% but market expectations would shift toward rate cuts later in 2025. Soft inflation + FOMC meeting June 18 (likely hold, but soft language) = tailwind for bonds. Entry on the CPI morning pop, ride the duration trade.
trades.catalyst: May 2025 CPI released June 11: headline 0.1% MoM (vs 0.2% est), core 0.1% MoM (vs 0.2% est). 2.4% YoY headline. Below consensus across all metrics. Bond futures turned positive immediately on print.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: TLT grinded from 82.71 → 83.7 on June 12 (soft PPI added to the bond bid). However, the Israel-Iran shock on June 13 created a confusing cross-current: safe-haven demand pushed bonds UP (flight to quality) but also raised inflation expectations (oil +7%). Net result: TLT was range-bound around 82-84 through mid-June, then drifted to 84.73 by June 30 as soft inflation data ultimately dominated. The trade worked but was slower and smaller than expected due to geopolitical oil shock creating inflation noise.
trades.lesson: Soft CPI was a genuine catalyst for bonds, but the Israel-Iran oil shock (June 13) created an offsetting inflationary impulse that capped the TLT upside. Geopolitical oil shocks are the #1 risk to bond longs — when you're long duration on disinflation and a war starts, oil price rises re-inflate breakevens and cap Treasury gains. Lesson: Size bond duration trades smaller when Middle East geopolitical risk is elevated. The trade worked (+2.5%) but took 3 weeks when the thesis expected 2 weeks.

trades.thesis: On June 12 (Thursday), Israel-Iran tensions were escalating (diplomatic talks breaking down, reports of Iranian uranium enrichment acceleration). Oil was already bid. Soft PPI on June 12 (0.1% vs 0.2% est) was actually good for the economy = good for oil demand. XOM is the highest-quality, lowest-cost oil major with $65/bbl breakeven. Even in a mild Iran escalation scenario, XOM benefits from higher oil prices while maintaining its dividend and buyback. If tensions escalate further, energy becomes a strategic long. Geopolitical call option on Iran conflict with quality operator downside protection.
trades.catalyst: Israel-Iran tensions escalating (pre-strike). Soft PPI supporting economic growth / oil demand. Oil already up from ~$67 to $70+ on geopolitical risk premium. Energy sector technical breakout (XOP breaking above 50-day MA).
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: MASSIVE win on the thesis. Israel struck Iran on June 13 (200 jets, killed IRGC leadership + nuclear scientists). Brent crude surged 7% on June 13 alone, another 0.5% on June 16. XOM went from $106.34 (entry June 12) → $108.66 (June 13) → $109.01 (June 16) → $110.48 (June 17). The strike also hit South Pars gas field on June 14, adding supply disruption. Strait of Hormuz closure fears (14% Polymarket probability) sustained the energy bid. Exited June 17 at $110.48 for +3.9% in 5 days.
trades.lesson: Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East should be traded as call options when you can identify the physical supply chain risk (Iran = 9th largest oil producer + Hormuz chokepoint). The setup on June 12 was: (1) escalating rhetoric, (2) energy technically breaking out, (3) oil already pricing in risk premium. XOM was the right vehicle — quality operator, dividend support, balance sheet strength. Pure E&P (DVN) would have been higher beta but also higher risk of a reversal. The 3.9% in 5 days was clean. Stayed too short — should have held to $115 target as geopolitical risk remained elevated through June 17.

trades.thesis: Israel strikes Iran June 13 — this is an explicit safe-haven catalyst. Gold is the cleanest geopolitical hedge. The 12-Day War starts today. Brent +7%, but gold also benefits as a non-correlated safe haven. Additionally, the soft CPI on June 11 weakens the dollar and provides a secular tailwind to gold. Geopolitical premium + dollar softening + rate-cut expectations = triple tailwind. Gold had already been in a structural bull market in 2025 (+50% YTD by year-end). Entry into the confirmed breakout on a genuine geopolitical shock.
trades.catalyst: Israel launches military strikes on Iran (June 13, 2025) — largest Middle East escalation in years. 200 jets hit 100+ targets including IRGC leadership and nuclear facilities. Strait of Hormuz closure risk (14% probability on Polymarket). Soft CPI weakening dollar backdrop.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: Got the direction right initially but bought the top of the geopolitical fear spike. GLD peaked at $316.29 on June 13 and then IMMEDIATELY sold off to $311.78 on June 16 as markets digested that the strikes were surgical (killing military/nuclear targets, not a full war), ceasefire negotiations were underway, and initial safe-haven panic was overdone. Entered on the day of peak fear — classic 'buy the rumor, sell the news' dynamic. The structural gold bull continued (GLD was ~$310 by year-end), but the specific entry point was the local top.
trades.lesson: NEVER buy gold on the day of a geopolitical shock — that's when everyone else is buying the fear spike. The better trade is to buy gold 2-3 days BEFORE the shock when tensions are rising but not at peak fear, OR to wait for the initial panic selloff after 'surgical strike = contained' news hits and buy the dip back into the structural trend. In this case the correct entry was June 12 ($312.2) or after the pullback on June 16-17 ($311-312). The June 13 entry at $316.29 was the local top. Rule: on acute geopolitical events, wait 1-2 days for price discovery before entering.

trades.thesis: AMD gapped up massively on June 16 (+8.8%, from $116.16 to $126.39) on reports that the US government approved AMD's MI308 GPU exports to China (case-by-case licensing guidance from BIS). This was a direct reversal of the April 2025 export restriction that cost AMD an estimated $800M charge. China AI demand for AMD GPUs is large (Alibaba $675M purchase reported). AMD is the only viable NVIDIA alternative for AI inference workloads — this approval re-opens a massive market. Entry on the gap-up confirmation with follow-through momentum expected.
trades.catalyst: BIS (Bureau of Industry and Security) approved AMD MI308 export licenses to China on case-by-case basis. Alibaba order (~$675M) reportedly coming through. AMD becoming credible NVIDIA alternative in Chinese AI market. Prior $800M charge now partially reversible.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AMD ran from $126.39 (June 16 entry) to $141.9 (June 30) = +12.3% in 14 days. The China export approval was a genuine fundamental re-rating: this effectively gave AMD access to the world's fastest-growing AI market. The stock continued to $162 by July 24 (+28.3% from entry). The gap-up on June 16 was a textbook 'buy the gap' setup on a fundamental catalyst — not a technical bounce. The broader AI infrastructure investment theme (AMD as NVIDIA alternative) also gained momentum throughout June.
trades.lesson: Policy reversals on export restrictions are high-conviction catalysts — they are binary events that change the addressable market. When a company gets $800M of revenue unblocked with a single policy change, the stock re-rates immediately and the momentum continues as order flow materializes. The correct sizing should have been larger (2-3x normal size) because the catalyst was clear, the risk/reward was exceptional (stop at $118 vs target $145 = R:R of 2.5:1 on a fundamental re-rating), and AMD was already technically strong. Holding to July 24 ($162) would have been even better (+28%). Lesson: policy reversal + AI tailwind + undiscovered catalyst = full position sizing.

trades.thesis: Small caps (IWM) had been lagging all week (IWM peaked at $212.22 on June 10, already declining). Israel-Iran war starts June 13 — small caps are the most domestically exposed, highest-debt, most rate-sensitive segment. Higher oil prices from the war would increase input costs for small businesses. The Kevin Warsh (new Fed chair) appointment was hawkish. Small caps have no AI buffer. In a geopolitical shock + hawkish Fed environment, IWM is the cleanest short: no AI story, high leverage, domestic exposure to oil cost increases.
trades.catalyst: Israel-Iran war starting June 13 (oil cost shock for small businesses). IWM technical breakdown from $212 high. Small-cap underperformance vs large cap had been persistent for weeks. Kevin Warsh as new Fed chair signaling hawkish tilt.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: IWM went from $206.66 (June 13) → $209.1 (June 16) → $206.9 (June 17). The short was basically flat (-0.1%). The thesis was correct directionally (small caps DID underperform, final IWM on June 17 was back near entry), but the execution was poor — the June 13 entry was at the post-shock low and the stock recovered on June 16 as markets stabilized after the initial panic. The broader 'Israel-Iran = risk-off for small caps' thesis was RIGHT but the TIMING was wrong — I should have entered the short BEFORE June 13 (June 10-12) when IWM was already rolling over from $212.
trades.lesson: Geopolitical events don't always break support — they often create a spike down followed by a quick recovery as initial fear is digested. The IWM short thesis was correct (small caps underperform in oil shocks + hawkish Fed environment) but the timing was late. The right entry was June 10-12 when the technical deterioration was already underway and before the war started. Post-war entry (June 13) was at the panic low which has worst risk/reward. Rule: short the weakness BEFORE the shock, not on the shock day. The IWM eventually did go lower (June 30 at $214 = actually rallied 3.5%!), so the structural thesis was wrong — small caps recovered as the geopolitical risk diminished and rate-cut expectations returned.

trades.thesis: Cloudflare (NET) held up well through the Israel-Iran shock (barely moved June 13-14). On June 16, the macro environment was improving: Israel-Iran ceasefire talks underway, soft CPI and PPI data still in effect, rate-cut expectations intact. NET is a pure AI/cybersecurity infrastructure play — every geopolitical event actually increases demand for network security (more state-sponsored cyberattacks during war periods). NET had been consolidating around $170-180 and looked ready to break out. The Israel-Iran war = more cybersecurity spending globally.
trades.catalyst: Post-Israel-Iran stability returning (ceasefire talks). Cybersecurity demand surge from geopolitical tensions (state-sponsored attack risk). Soft macro data (CPI/PPI) supporting growth stocks. Technical consolidation breakout setup at $179.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: NET ran from $179.27 to $195.83 (+9.2%) in 14 days. The geopolitical cyber spending thesis plus soft macro data created a perfect setup. NET eventually reached $192.85 by July 7. The stock was under-owned relative to the AI infrastructure theme (most attention was on NVDA/AVGO/AMD) and the cybersecurity angle from geopolitical tensions was not widely priced in. This was a genuinely differentiated thesis in a crowded AI trade.
trades.lesson: Geopolitical conflicts create second-order effects that are less obvious than the direct plays (oil/gold). Israel-Iran war → increased state-sponsored cyberattack risk → higher cybersecurity budget urgency → NET, PANW, CRWD benefit. The market was focused on the obvious plays (XOM, GLD) while ignoring the indirect beneficiaries. Finding the non-consensus angle within a macro theme often yields better risk-adjusted returns than the crowded obvious play (GLD on June 13 = crowded, top). Second-order geopolitical beneficiaries are consistently underpriced.

period_summary.note_on_sizing: All trades assumed equal weight (1/8 portfolio each). In practice, AMD and AVGO should have been 2x size (policy reversal + earnings beat + fundamental re-rating = highest conviction). GLD and IWM should have been 0.5x (timing-dependent geopolitical trades with lower conviction).
dominant_themes: 1. SOFT CPI (June 11): 0.1% MoM vs 0.2% expected — rate-cut optionality returned, AI growth stocks bid. Best expressed via AVGO, MSFT, ARM.

2. ISRAEL-IRAN 12-DAY WAR (June 13): The defining event. 200 Israeli jets hit 100+ Iranian targets including nuclear sites. Brent +7% on June 13. Oil/energy was the direct play (XOM, XOP, USO surged). Gold spiked then pulled back (don't buy geopolitical gold on the day of the shock). IWM and AAPL sold off. Ceasefire talks started within days, capping further escalation.

3. AMD CHINA EXPORT APPROVAL (June 16): BIS approved MI308 licenses to China — reversed the April $800M charge. AMD gapped +8.8% and continued to run. Policy reversal catalysts are the cleanest fundamental re-ratings.
4. AVGO POST-EARNINGS DRIFT: AVGO reported June 5 (Q2: record $15B rev, AI +46% YoY). The drift continued through June — custom AI chip moat being re-rated by market.
5. ENERGY MEGA-MOVE: XOP +6.9% in 4 days (June 10-13), USO +14.9% in 7 days (June 10-17). The biggest sector trade of the week. Driven entirely by Israel-Iran geopolitical risk premium.
what_market_got_right: Energy was the cleanest geopolitical play — XOM, XOP, USO surged on legitimate supply risk (Iran = 9th largest oil producer + Hormuz control)
AI infrastructure (AVGO, NVDA, ARM, TSM) was resilient through the geopolitical shock — institutional hands were buying the dip
AMD re-rating on China policy reversal was swift and legitimate — market correctly identified this as a fundamental TAM expansion
Small caps (IWM) lagged as expected in a higher-oil-cost + hawkish-Fed environment
what_market_got_wrong: Gold spiked too hard on June 13 and gave back gains — initial geopolitical panic was overdone as strikes turned out to be surgical not existential
Broader market (SPY) dipped only -1.1% on June 13 then fully recovered — fear was well-contained. The AI/tech secular trend was stronger than the geopolitical headwind
key_lessons_for_future_periods: GEOPOLITICAL TRADE TIMING: Enter energy longs BEFORE the conflict event (June 10-12 when tensions visible), NOT on the day of strikes (June 13 = local top for energy)
SAFE HAVEN TIMING: Same rule applies to gold — buy on escalating tensions not on the confirmed strike day. GLD peaked exactly on June 13.

POLICY REVERSAL = HIGHEST CONVICTION: AMD's China export approval was a binary policy event re-opening a massive market. These are the highest-quality fundamental catalysts — size up aggressively (2-3x normal).

POST-EARNINGS DRIFT IN AI STRUCTURAL WINNERS: AVGO's June 5 earnings created a drift that lasted weeks. AI infrastructure companies with accelerating growth + raising guidance = buy and hold through volatility.
CYBERSECURITY AS SECOND-ORDER GEOPOLITICAL PLAY: NET/PANW/CRWD benefit from geopolitical conflict via increased state-sponsored cyberattack risk. Less crowded than oil/gold direct plays.

SHORT THE RELATIVE WEAKNESS NOT THE INDEX: AAPL as a short (no AI story, China supply risk, expensive) outperformed shorting SPY or IWM outright. Sector rotation shorts on non-participants are cleaner than index shorts.

ENERGY REVERSAL AWARENESS: XOP peaked at $131.67 on June 17 and fell back to $123.55 by June 30 (-6%). Oil geopolitical spikes are temporary unless Hormuz actually closes. Energy longs should be quick trades (5-7 days) not swing trades.

sources_and_verification.cpi_data: CNBC June 11 2025: 'CPI inflation report May 2025: U.S. inflation rises 0.1%' — headline 2.4% YoY, core 2.8% YoY, both MoM below expectations
sources_and_verification.israel_iran_war: Britannica '12-Day War (June 2025)'; Al Jazeera June 13 2025; Julius Baer Israel-Iran conflict market impact report — Brent +7% on June 13, oil futures spiked 13%+ at peak
sources_and_verification.avgo_earnings: Broadcom Q2 FY2025 earnings call transcript June 5 2025 (Motley Fool/Nasdaq) — record $15B revenue, AI revenue $4.4B +46% YoY, Q3 guidance $15.8B
sources_and_verification.amd_china_approval: Investing.com/Yahoo Finance: BIS approved AMD MI308 export licenses; Alibaba ~$675M order reported; prior $800M charge on April 15 2025 (CNBC)
sources_and_verification.fomc_context: IndMoney: 'Fed Rate Cut News: Fed Keeps Rates Steady in June 2025' — June 18 FOMC hold at 4.25-4.5%, fourth consecutive hold
sources_and_verification.ppi_data: Globe and Mail June 13 2025 — PPI increased 0.1% in May vs 0.2% consensus, core PPI 0.2% vs 0.3% consensus
sources_and_verification.price_data_source: yfinance historical data, pulled directly — all entry/exit prices verified against actual historical closes
